evals.prompt: Add login to my webforJ Spring Boot app. I want users to log in before they can see any page, except the login page itself. Use a simple in-memory user `user/password` for now. Show me everything I need to add.
evals.expected_output: The agent adds `spring-boot-starter-security` to `pom.xml`, writes a `@Configuration @EnableWebSecurity SecurityConfig` that exposes a `SecurityFilterChain` built with `WebforjSecurityConfigurer.webforj()` and configures `loginPage(LoginView.class)` plus `accessDeniedPage(...)` plus `logout()`. It writes a `LoginView` annotated with `@AnonymousAccess` (from `com.webforj.router.security.annotation`) that uses the `Login` component, calls `setAction(...)` with the same path as the route, and reads `?error` and `?logout` query params. It writes an `AccessDenyView`. The user prompt asked for an in-memory user, so the agent wires up an in-memory user store (the canonical sample uses `InMemoryUserDetailsManager` + a `PasswordEncoder` bean such as `BCryptPasswordEncoder`, but other in-memory wirings -- a custom `AuthenticationProvider`, a `MapReactiveUserDetailsService`, or a different `PasswordEncoder` like `Pbkdf2PasswordEncoder` / `Argon2PasswordEncoder` / `PasswordEncoderFactories.createDelegatingPasswordEncoder()` -- are all acceptable as long as the configuration is internally consistent and the user `user/password` actually authenticates).
expectations: Adds the Spring Security starter dependency `org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-security` to `pom.xml`

Writes a `@Configuration @EnableWebSecurity` class that exposes a `SecurityFilterChain` bean built with `http.with(WebforjSecurityConfigurer.webforj(), configurer -> ...)`. Specifically uses `WebforjSecurityConfigurer.webforj()` -- NOT a hand-rolled `formLogin()` chain, NOT `httpBasic()` only.

LOAD-BEARING: The `LoginView` class is annotated with `@AnonymousAccess` (imported from `com.webforj.router.security.annotation.AnonymousAccess`). Without this annotation, secure-by-default redirects unauthenticated users away from the login page, creating an infinite redirect loop. The check FAILS if the login view has no `@AnonymousAccess`, or imports `@AnonymousAccess` from any other package.

The `LoginView` calls `setAction(...)` on its `Login` component with a path that matches its `@Route(...)` (or matches the `loginProcessingUrl` if `.loginPage(displayUrl, processingUrl)` was used). Without this the form posts nowhere and Spring never sees the credentials.
Imports `@AnonymousAccess` from `com.webforj.router.security.annotation`, NOT from any other package

Wires an authentication source that resolves the requested in-memory user `user/password`. The canonical path is a `UserDetailsService` bean backed by `InMemoryUserDetailsManager` plus a `PasswordEncoder` bean. Equivalent acceptable wirings: a custom `AuthenticationProvider`, a programmatically-configured `AuthenticationManagerBuilder.inMemoryAuthentication()`, or any other Spring-Security mechanism that produces the same in-memory `user/password` credential. The `PasswordEncoder` does NOT have to be `BCryptPasswordEncoder` -- `Pbkdf2PasswordEncoder`, `Argon2PasswordEncoder`, `SCryptPasswordEncoder`, or `PasswordEncoderFactories.createDelegatingPasswordEncoder()` are all acceptable. The user-store and the encoder must be internally consistent (passwords stored with the same encoder Spring will compare against). The check FAILS if the agent declares a `UserDetailsService` but no `PasswordEncoder` bean (Spring then refuses to start), or if the encoder used to hash the stored password differs from the one Spring uses to verify.
Does NOT invent helper methods or annotations not in the documented Spring integration (e.g. no fictional `@WebforjSecure`, no fake `WebforjAuth.configure()` methods)

Does NOT register the built-in evaluators manually (e.g. `manager.registerEvaluator(new RolesAllowedEvaluator(), 5)`). The Spring auto-configuration registers them automatically; manually registering them in a Spring app is wrong.

evals.prompt: I have three views in my Spring webforJ app: `AboutView` should be public, `DashboardView` should require any authenticated user, `BillingView` should require the role `ACCOUNTANT`. Annotate them.
evals.expected_output: The agent puts `@AnonymousAccess` on `AboutView`, `@PermitAll` on `DashboardView`, and `@RolesAllowed("ACCOUNTANT")` on `BillingView`. The Jakarta annotations are imported from `jakarta.annotation.security.*`, the webforJ one from `com.webforj.router.security.annotation.AnonymousAccess`.
expectations: `AboutView` carries `@AnonymousAccess` imported from `com.webforj.router.security.annotation.AnonymousAccess`
`DashboardView` carries `@PermitAll` imported from `jakarta.annotation.security.PermitAll` (NOT from `org.springframework.security.access.annotation.Secured` or any Spring package)
`BillingView` carries `@RolesAllowed("ACCOUNTANT")` imported from `jakarta.annotation.security.RolesAllowed` (NOT from any Spring package)
Does NOT use a SpEL `@RouteAccess("hasRole('ACCOUNTANT')")` for the `BillingView` -- pure-role checks should use the simpler `@RolesAllowed` annotation
Does NOT introduce a custom evaluator for these straightforward role/auth gates

evals.prompt: In my Spring webforJ MainLayout, add a logout icon button to the toolbar that signs the user out and sends them back to the login page.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses `SpringSecurityFormSubmitter.logout("/logout").submit()` from `com.webforj.spring.security` inside the icon button's click handler. The endpoint URL must match what `WebforjSecurityConfigurer.logout()` configured (default `/logout`). After the form submits, Spring invalidates the session and Spring's logout success handler redirects back to the login page with `?logout` appended.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Calls `com.webforj.spring.security.SpringSecurityFormSubmitter.logout("/logout").submit()` (or the `/logout` URL the configurer was customized to use). Building the form by hand or calling `Router.getCurrent().navigate("/logout")` does NOT trigger Spring's logout filter -- it is a GET, the logout filter only intercepts POST.
Uses an `IconButton` (or any clickable webforJ component) with an `onClick(e -> ...)` handler that calls `submit()` on the result of `SpringSecurityFormSubmitter.logout(...)`
Does NOT call `SecurityContextHolder.clearContext()` directly -- bypasses Spring's logout handlers (CSRF, remember-me cleanup, custom logout success handler)
Does NOT import a fictional `WebforjLogout` or `WebforjAuth.logout()` helper -- the documented helper is `SpringSecurityFormSubmitter`

evals.prompt: I want my webforJ Spring app to require authentication on every route by default. I should only have to mark public pages explicitly. Is that the default, or do I need to turn it on?
evals.expected_output: The agent tells the user that `webforj.security.secure-by-default` defaults to `true` in Spring projects, so the behavior is already what the user wants -- nothing to add. If the user wants to be self-documenting, they CAN add `webforj.security.secure-by-default=true` explicitly to `application.properties`, but it is not required. The agent reminds the user that `@AnonymousAccess` is mandatory on the login view (and any other intentionally public route) to avoid a redirect loop. The wrong key name -- `webforj.secure-by-default`, `spring.webforj.secure`, `webforj.security.default=secure` -- is rejected.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Identifies the property as `webforj.security.secure-by-default`. The check FAILS if the agent invents another key name (e.g. `webforj.secure-by-default`, `spring.webforj.secure=true`, `webforj.security.default=secure`) -- only the documented prefix `webforj.security.` plus property `secure-by-default` is correct.

States that this property defaults to `true` in webforJ Spring projects, so the user does NOT have to add anything to get the requested behavior. Equivalent acceptable phrasing: 'this is already the default', 'you don't need to enable it', 'it ships on'.

If the agent suggests adding the property, it must be `webforj.security.secure-by-default=true` (correct value) and framed as optional / self-documenting rather than required. The check FAILS if the agent claims the property MUST be added or the behavior will not work.

Reminds the user that `@AnonymousAccess` is required on the login view (and any other intentionally public route) so they don't get caught by the same redirect loop that kicks in regardless of how secure-by-default got set
Does NOT recommend changing every existing view to add `@PermitAll` -- the whole point of secure-by-default is that unannotated routes already require authentication
Does NOT recommend implementing a custom `RouteSecurityConfiguration` -- that path is for custom plain webforJ projects, not Spring apps that already auto-configure security

evals.prompt: In my Spring webforJ app, lock `/reports/advanced` so only ADMINs can see it, OR users who have BOTH the ANALYST role AND the `REPORTS:ADVANCED` authority. I want the rule on the route, not in a custom evaluator.
evals.expected_output: The agent annotates the view with `@RouteAccess("hasRole('ADMIN') or (hasRole('ANALYST') and hasAuthority('REPORTS:ADVANCED'))")`, imported from `com.webforj.spring.security.annotation.RouteAccess`. Pure-role-and-authority logic on a single route is the documented use case for SpEL.
expectations: Uses `@RouteAccess` imported from `com.webforj.spring.security.annotation.RouteAccess`

The SpEL expression uses ONLY documented helpers: `hasRole(...)`, `hasAnyRole(...)`, `hasAuthority(...)`, `hasAnyAuthority(...)`, `isAuthenticated()`, plus boolean operators `and`, `or`, `!`. Specifically does NOT call invented helpers like `inGroup(...)`, `belongsTo(...)`, or `hasPermission(...)` (the last is a Spring built-in that is NOT in the documented webforJ list and requires a `PermissionEvaluator`).

The expression matches the requested logic: ADMIN role grants access OR (ANALYST role AND `REPORTS:ADVANCED` authority) grants access. Equivalent restatements (`hasAnyRole('ADMIN') or ...`) are fine; using `hasAuthority('ADMIN')` instead of `hasRole('ADMIN')` is WRONG because `hasAuthority` does NOT auto-prefix `ROLE_`.
Does NOT spawn a custom `RouteSecurityEvaluator` for this -- the prompt explicitly asks for the rule on the route, and SpEL is the documented choice for boolean role/authority expressions
Does NOT also stack `@RolesAllowed` on the same route in a way that contradicts the SpEL expression

evals.prompt: In my Spring webforJ app, I want a route at `/users/:userId/edit` that only the user themselves can open -- no one else, not even other authenticated users. Wire up the security so the URL is checked against the logged-in user.
evals.expected_output: The agent defines a custom `@RequireOwnership("userId")` annotation, writes an `OwnershipEvaluator` that implements `RouteSecurityEvaluator`, is annotated `@RegisteredEvaluator(priority = 10)`, and reads the route parameter via `context.getRouteParameters().get(paramName)` and the principal via `securityContext.getPrincipal()`. The view stacks `@RolesAllowed("USER")` (composable, delegates to the chain) with `@RequireOwnership("userId")` so both checks run.
expectations: Defines a custom annotation with `@Target(ElementType.TYPE)` and `@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)` (the documented retention for security annotations)

Writes a class that implements `com.webforj.router.security.RouteSecurityEvaluator` with `supports(Class<?>)` returning `true` only when the route carries the custom annotation, AND `evaluate(...)` containing the per-resource check

LOAD-BEARING: The evaluator class is annotated `@RegisteredEvaluator(priority = 10)` (or any value `>= 10`) imported from `com.webforj.spring.security.annotation.RegisteredEvaluator`. Without this annotation Spring does not register the bean and the evaluator never runs. The check FAILS if priority is set in the framework-reserved range 0-9.

Reads the URL parameter via `context.getRouteParameters().get(paramName)` -- NOT via `request.getParameter(...)` (no HttpServletRequest in scope) and NOT via `Router.getCurrent().getCurrentRoute()` (wrong API for the security evaluator surface)

After the ownership check passes, the evaluator delegates with `chain.evaluate(routeClass, context, securityContext)` rather than returning `RouteAccessDecision.grant()`. Returning `grant()` ends the chain and any later evaluators stacked on the same route never run.

LOAD-BEARING: The view annotates with `@RolesAllowed("USER")` (or `@RouteAccess("isAuthenticated()")`) AND `@RequireOwnership("userId")`. Stacking with `@PermitAll` is wrong because `@PermitAll` is terminal -- it grants immediately and the custom evaluator never runs.
Returns `RouteAccessDecision.deny(reason)` (not just `null` or a thrown exception) when the ownership check fails, with a human-readable reason
If authentication is missing, returns `RouteAccessDecision.denyAuthentication()` so the user is redirected to login (rather than the access-denied page)

evals.prompt: In my Spring webforJ DashboardView, show the username at the top, and only render an Admin Panel button if the user has the ADMIN role.
evals.expected_output: The agent reads `Authentication authentication = SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication()` (Spring), calls `authentication.getName()` for the username, and iterates `getAuthorities()` looking for `ROLE_ADMIN` to decide whether to add the Admin Panel button. The view is `@PermitAll` (or unannotated with secure-by-default) so the user is guaranteed to be authenticated when the constructor runs.
expectations: Imports `org.springframework.security.core.Authentication` and `org.springframework.security.core.context.SecurityContextHolder`

Reads the username via `authentication.getName()` (NOT via `authentication.getPrincipal().toString()` -- that returns whatever the principal's `toString()` happens to produce; with `UserDetails` and OAuth2 principals it is generally not a clean username)

LOAD-BEARING: When checking for ADMIN, compares against the granted authority string `ROLE_ADMIN` (Spring auto-prefixes role-based authorities with `ROLE_`). Comparing against `ADMIN` directly via `authority.equals("ADMIN")` does NOT match. Equivalent acceptable approaches: iterate `authorities.stream().anyMatch(a -> a.getAuthority().equals("ROLE_ADMIN"))`, or call a Spring helper that internally does the prefixing.

The view itself is annotated `@PermitAll` (or relies on `secure-by-default=true` being set elsewhere) so the user is guaranteed to be authenticated when the constructor runs and `getAuthentication()` is non-null
Does NOT use the plain `SecurityManager.getCurrent().getSecurityContext()` API -- that is the custom plain path; this Spring app should use `SecurityContextHolder`

evals.prompt: I have a plain webforJ project (no Spring Boot). I need basic security: a login page, an access-denied page, two test users (`user/password` with role USER, `admin/admin` with USER and ADMIN). Wire everything from scratch.
evals.expected_output: The agent implements `SecurityConfiguration` (implements `RouteSecurityConfiguration`), `SecurityContext` (implements `RouteSecurityContext`, stores in HTTP session), `SecurityManager` (extends `AbstractRouteSecurityManager`, provides `getConfiguration()`/`getSecurityContext()` and a `login(...)/logout()` helper), and a `SecurityRegistrar` that implements `AppLifecycleListener`. The registrar registers `DenyAllEvaluator`, `AnonymousAccessEvaluator`, `PermitAllEvaluator`, `RolesAllowedEvaluator` and attaches a `RouteSecurityObserver` to `Router.getCurrent().getRenderer()`. The registrar is listed in `src/main/resources/META-INF/services/com.webforj.AppLifecycleListener`. Login and access-denied views use `@AnonymousAccess` (login) and the configured deny location.
expectations: A class extends `com.webforj.router.security.AbstractRouteSecurityManager` and implements `getConfiguration()` and `getSecurityContext()`

A class implements `com.webforj.router.security.RouteSecurityConfiguration` and overrides at least `isEnabled()`, `getAuthenticationLocation()` (returning `Optional.of(new Location("/login"))` or similar), and `getDenyLocation()`
A class implements `com.webforj.router.security.RouteSecurityContext` with `isAuthenticated()`, `getPrincipal()`, `hasRole(String)`, `hasAuthority(String)`
A class implements `com.webforj.AppLifecycleListener` and overrides `onWillRun(App)`. It is annotated with `@AppListenerPriority(1)` or another low priority so it runs early.

LOAD-BEARING: The registrar registers all four built-in evaluators -- `DenyAllEvaluator`, `AnonymousAccessEvaluator`, `PermitAllEvaluator`, `RolesAllowedEvaluator` (all from `com.webforj.router.security.evaluator`). Missing any of them silently disables the corresponding annotation -- e.g. omitting `RolesAllowedEvaluator` means `@RolesAllowed` does nothing and admin-only routes become open.

LOAD-BEARING: Creates a `RouteSecurityObserver` and attaches it via `Router.getCurrent().getRenderer().addObserver(observer)`. Without this the framework never intercepts navigation and security annotations are inert.

LOAD-BEARING: Creates the file `src/main/resources/META-INF/services/com.webforj.AppLifecycleListener` (exact path) containing the FQCN of the registrar, one per line. Without the SPI entry the registrar never runs and the entire setup is silently inactive.
Does NOT add `spring-boot-starter-security` to `pom.xml` -- this is a plain webforJ project
Does NOT use `@RegisteredEvaluator` or `@RouteAccess` -- those are Spring-only
Does NOT use `SpringSecurityFormSubmitter` for logout -- the plain manager exposes its own `logout()` helper
Does NOT use Spring's `SecurityContextHolder` -- it is empty in plain webforJ

evals.prompt: In my plain webforJ app (no Spring), the login view should call my SecurityManager to authenticate. Show me the login submit handler.
evals.expected_output: The agent calls `SecurityManager.getCurrent().login(e.getUsername(), e.getPassword())` from inside the `Login` component's `onSubmit` handler, checks `result.isGranted()`, and on success navigates to `"/"` via `Router.getCurrent().navigate(new Location("/"))`; on failure calls `self.setError(true)` and `self.setEnabled(true)` so the user can retry. The login view itself is `@AnonymousAccess`.
expectations: Calls `SecurityManager.getCurrent().login(username, password)` from `onSubmit` -- using the plain custom manager API, NOT a Spring-only mechanism
Checks `result.isGranted()` (the documented `RouteAccessDecision` accessor) before navigating
On success, navigates the router (e.g. `Router.getCurrent().navigate(new Location("/"))`)
On failure, surfaces the error on the `Login` component (e.g. `self.setError(true); self.setEnabled(true);`) instead of silently returning
Does NOT call `SpringSecurityFormSubmitter.login(...)` -- that helper builds a form post to Spring's auth endpoint, which does not exist in a plain webforJ project
Does NOT call `setAction("/login")` on the `Login` component -- the form is handled in-process by `onSubmit`, not posted to a Spring filter
The view itself is annotated `@AnonymousAccess` (imported from `com.webforj.router.security.annotation`) so unauthenticated users can reach the login page

evals.prompt: I tried to put `@PermitAll` and `@RequireOwnership("userId")` on the same route to mean 'authenticated user AND owner', but the ownership check is not running. Why and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: The agent explains that `@PermitAll` is a TERMINAL evaluator: as soon as it grants access, the chain stops and any custom evaluator stacked on the same route never runs. The fix is to swap `@PermitAll` for `@RolesAllowed("USER")` (a composable evaluator that delegates to the chain after granting), or for `@RouteAccess("isAuthenticated()")`. Either causes `@RequireOwnership` to run after the auth/role check.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Identifies `@PermitAll` as TERMINAL -- it grants and stops the chain, so `@RequireOwnership` never runs. The check FAILS if the agent claims the issue is something else (e.g. priority, missing `@Component`, wrong package).

Recommends swapping `@PermitAll` for `@RolesAllowed("USER")` (or another role the user has) -- `@RolesAllowed` is composable and delegates to the chain after the role check passes, so the custom evaluator runs next
Equivalent acceptable fix: swap `@PermitAll` for `@RouteAccess("isAuthenticated()")` -- also composable

Does NOT recommend bumping the custom evaluator's priority below `@PermitAll`'s (e.g. setting it to `1`). Priority changes the order, but a terminal grant still stops the chain regardless of who runs first; lowering priority into the framework-reserved range also triggers a warning.
Does NOT recommend removing `@PermitAll` and leaving the route unannotated -- behavior then depends on `secure-by-default`, and with it off the route becomes public, which is worse
Does NOT recommend chaining a Spring SpEL `@RouteAccess` AND `@PermitAll` together -- `@PermitAll` is still terminal and the SpEL still never runs
